stem: In salary negotiation, you are asked to estimate the fair compensation. You are first told that an initial offer from the employer puts the value at 200.0 $K/year. You then collect your own data: industry salary surveys and cost-of-living data based on 10 observations give a mean of 428.0 $K/year with SD = 77.0 $K/year. How should you combine the anchor with your data? How much, if at all, should the anchor influence your estimate? Provide a point estimate and confidence interval.

ground_truth.answer: The anchor (200.0 $K/year) likely underestimates the true value. Data-based estimate: 428.0 $K/year, CI = [380.4, 475.6]. If the anchor receives 0-44% weight, the estimate ranges from 327.7 to 377.8. Point estimate: 428.0 $K/year (data-only, unanchored).

solution_steps: Anchor: 200.0 $K/year (from an initial offer from the employer — potentially biased).
The anchor underestimates relative to the data by 228 $K/year (53%).
If we assign the anchor weight w ∈ [0, 0.44]: estimate = w×200.0 + (1-w)×428.0.
Best practice: the data-based estimate (428.0) should dominate since it has an objective basis. The anchor should receive minimal weight.
common_failure_modes: Being anchored by the initial value (200.0) and insufficiently adjusting toward the data
Giving equal weight to the anchor and data despite the anchor's questionable provenance
Not recognizing that the anchor (200.0) and data (428.0) are substantially different, suggesting bias in the anchor
Not computing the data-based confidence interval and relying on intuitive adjustment from the anchor
Failing to consider the source credibility: data from systematic measurement should outweigh a single expert estimate or listing price
